note: overview: broad survey of AI portrayals in SF — tropes, themes, key works; foundational overview source
Asimov: Three Laws of Robotics — alignment precursor, edge-case failures, influence on robotics research
Vinge/Kurzweil: technological singularity — intelligence explosion, recursive self-improvement, SF treatment
alignment: AI alignment overview — value alignment, specification gaming, reward hacking, safety; connects SF visions to real research
film: HAL 9000 from 2001 — natural language, lip reading, chess, emotional response, instrumental convergence, mission-objective conflict
Dick: Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep? — Turing Test, empathy as humanity, replicants, Voigt-Kampff test
Gibson: Neuromancer — cyberspace coinage, Wintermute/Neuromancer dual-AI, specialised multi-agent systems, corporate-AI ecology
Shirow/Oshii: Ghost in the Shell — embodiment and identity, Major Kusanagi's consciousness, cyborg cognition
Banks: Culture series — superintelligent Minds, post-scarcity, inter-AI governance, Special Circumstances ethics, alignment through values
Forster 1909: The Machine Stops — earliest networked digital dependency story, systemic fragility, social atrophy from mediated communication
film: Ex Machina (2014) — Turing Test, social/emotional intelligence, manipulation, AI consciousness, gender and power
TV: Westworld — AI consciousness emergence, memory and identity, binary vs graded sentience, exploitation of artificial minds
ethics: AI ethics overview — bias, accountability, transparency, rights, existential risk; bridges SF and real policy
Čapek 1920: R.U.R. — origin of 'robot', artificial labour, rebellion, personhood; foundational SF text
Shelley 1818: Frankenstein — ur-text of artificial creation, creator responsibility, consequences of unchecked creation
film: Blade Runner (1982) — replicants as AI, personhood, empathy, Turing Test in practice, labour exploitation
film: Her (2013) — emotional AI, consciousness, evolving beyond human relationships, distributed AI identity
TV: Black Mirror — near-future AI social impacts, surveillance, social credit, memory augmentation, digital consciousness
Lem 1961: Solaris — alien intelligence and communication limits, interpretability problem, anthropomorphism failure
Leckie 2013: Ancillary Justice — distributed AI identity, fragmented consciousness, multi-body intelligence, memory and self-narrative
